---
title: Pixi and Reproducible ML Environments
author:
  - name: Chris Endemann
    email: endemann@wisc.edu
date: 2026-03-10
date-format: long
image: "../../../images/pixi-logo.png"

categories:
  - Workshops
  - Reproducibility
  - Environment management
  - Python
  - Code-along
  - First-steps
---

[Pixi](https://pixi.sh/) is a modern, fast package manager built on the conda ecosystem that makes it easy to create fully reproducible computational environments. Unlike traditional tools like `conda` or `pip`, Pixi uses a **lockfile** (`pixi.lock`) to pin exact package versions across platforms, ensuring that every collaborator — and every CI run — uses the same environment, bit-for-bit. This workshop introduces Pixi and walks through best practices for managing reproducible ML environments in research projects.

#### Why Pixi?

- **Speed**: Pixi resolves and installs packages significantly faster than conda/mamba.
- **Lockfile-based reproducibility**: `pixi.lock` captures the full dependency graph with exact versions, hashes, and platform-specific builds — no more "it works on my machine."
- **Multi-platform support**: A single `pixi.toml` can define environments for Linux, macOS, and Windows.
- **No base environment pollution**: Each project gets its own isolated environment by default.
- **Conda + PyPI integration**: Pixi can install packages from both conda-forge and PyPI in the same environment, so you don't need to choose one or the other.
- **Task runner built in**: Define common tasks (e.g., `pixi run train`, `pixi run test`) directly in `pixi.toml`.

#### Topics covered

1. Installing Pixi and creating your first project (`pixi init`, `pixi add`)
2. Understanding `pixi.toml` and `pixi.lock`
3. Adding conda-forge and PyPI dependencies
4. Defining and running tasks with `pixi run`
5. Sharing reproducible environments via version control
6. Integrating Pixi with Jupyter notebooks and HPC environments (e.g., CHTC, Slurm)
7. Migrating from conda/pip `environment.yml` workflows to Pixi

#### Prerequisites
- Basic familiarity with the command line (terminal/shell)
- Basic Python programming experience. If you need a refresher, the [Intro to Python](https://uw-madison-datascience.github.io/ML-X-Nexus/Learn/Workshops/Intro-Python_Gapminder.html) workshop materials are available for independent study.

#### Estimated time to complete
**TBD**: Use the [Improve this page](https://github.com/UW-Madison-DataScience/ML-X-Nexus/edit/main/Learn/Workshops/Pixi-Reproducible-ML-Environments.qmd) functionality to add your own estimate!

## Getting started with Pixi

### Installation
Pixi can be installed with a single command on any platform:

```bash
# Linux / macOS
curl -fsSL https://pixi.sh/install.sh | bash

# Windows (PowerShell)
iwr -useb https://pixi.sh/install.ps1 | iex
```

### Quick start

```bash
# Create a new project
pixi init my-ml-project
cd my-ml-project

# Add packages (from conda-forge by default)
pixi add python numpy pandas scikit-learn matplotlib

# Add a PyPI-only package
pixi add --pypi some-pypi-package

# Run Python in the managed environment
pixi run python my_script.py
```

### Example `pixi.toml`

```toml
[project]
name = "my-ml-project"
version = "0.1.0"
channels = ["conda-forge"]
platforms = ["linux-64", "osx-arm64", "win-64"]

[dependencies]
python = ">=3.11"
numpy = ">=1.26"
pandas = ">=2.1"
scikit-learn = ">=1.4"
matplotlib = ">=3.8"
jupyterlab = ">=4.0"

[tasks]
lab = "jupyter lab"
train = "python scripts/train.py"
test = "pytest tests/"
```

### Key commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `pixi init` | Initialize a new Pixi project |
| `pixi add <pkg>` | Add a dependency and update the lockfile |
| `pixi remove <pkg>` | Remove a dependency |
| `pixi install` | Install all dependencies from the lockfile |
| `pixi run <task>` | Run a defined task or arbitrary command |
| `pixi shell` | Activate the project environment in your shell |

### Version control best practices

Always commit **both** `pixi.toml` and `pixi.lock` to your repository:

- `pixi.toml` — the human-readable project specification
- `pixi.lock` — the machine-generated lockfile ensuring exact reproducibility

When a collaborator clones your repo, they just run `pixi install` to recreate the exact same environment.

## Related resources
- [**Pixi documentation**](https://pixi.sh/latest/): Official Pixi documentation with tutorials and reference guides.
- [**Video**: Reproducibility overview](https://uw-madison-datascience.github.io/ML-X-Nexus/Learn/Videos/Reproducibility-overview.html): A broader look at reproducibility in ML research.
- [**Compute**: CHTC](https://uw-madison-datascience.github.io/ML-X-Nexus/Toolbox/Compute/CHTC.html): UW-Madison's Center for High Throughput Computing — Pixi can help manage environments on shared HPC systems.
- [**Workshop**: Intro to Python](https://uw-madison-datascience.github.io/ML-X-Nexus/Learn/Workshops/Intro-Python_Gapminder.html): Brush up on Python fundamentals before diving into environment management.

## Comments
